title: The Law That Makes Dissent a Crime Against God: Iran's Rule of the Jurist in 2026
quick_answer: Iran's Velayat-e Faqih doctrine defines the ruling cleric as God's designated authority, so dissent becomes a crime against God, not the state. Under Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, installed in March 2026 after his father's assassination, executions under President Pezeshkian have passed 3,920, and 24-year-old Arghavan Fallahi now faces death for 'baghi' — armed rebellion — after a family road trip.
article_body:
On July 1, 2026, a lawyer in Tehran received formal notice that Branch 15 of the Revolutionary Court had sentenced her client, 24-year-old Arghavan Fallahi, to death. The charge was baghi — armed rebellion against the Islamic state — a category of crime that Iran's Islamic Penal Code inherits directly from religious jurisprudence, where taking up arms against a legitimate Islamic ruler is treated as rebellion against the order God himself established, according to IranWire. Judge Abolghasem Salavati, sanctioned by the European Union, the United States and the United Kingdom for his role in systematic human rights violations, signed the verdict. Fallahi's documented offense, according to Hrana, was traveling from Isfahan to Shiraz in November 2022 with her father, Nasrallah, and brother, Ardavan, allegedly to meet members of a banned opposition group. She had already served two years for "assembly and collusion" before this second, capital charge arrived. Under Iran's Velayat-e Faqih system, a family road trip and a closed-door meeting are enough to become a crime against God. That is not rhetorical framing. It is the literal architecture of the law.

What is Velayat-e Faqih, and why does it turn dissent into theology?

Velayat-e Faqih — "guardianship of the Islamic jurist" — is the doctrine Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini built Iran's post-1979 state on: that in the absence of the Twelfth Imam, a supreme jurist rules as his deputy, exercising authority that traces, in the regime's own account, back to the Prophet. The doctrine does not present the Supreme Leader as a head of state subject to political consent. It presents him as the executor of divine will. That premise does the legal work that ordinary authoritarian systems need secret police and show trials to fake: it converts opposition to the government into opposition to God's order on earth, and Iran's courts apply charges — baghi, moharebeh ("waging war against God") — that exist nowhere in a secular criminal code. A protester is not merely breaking a law passed by fallible legislators. In the state's own legal language, she is warring against heaven.

Who inherited "God's rule" after Ali Khamenei was killed?

On February 28, 2026, Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was killed in a joint U.S.–Israeli strike on his Tehran compound, according to Bloomberg and the Washington Post; Iranian state media confirmed the death and declared forty days of national mourning. Khamenei had reportedly given his closest advisers a shortlist of three possible successors — his son Mojtaba was not on it, according to the Irish Times. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps pushed anyway. On March 3, an initial Assembly of Experts vote delivered Mojtaba Khamenei the two-thirds majority he needed, over open objections from some members that hereditary succession violated the republic's own founding logic. He was formally installed as Iran's third Supreme Leader on March 8, 2026. Analysts at the Stimson Center and the Gulf International Forum describe the transition not as continuity but as a shift toward open IRGC dominance — a guardianship of the jurist increasingly guaranteed by men with guns rather than by theological consensus.

What does the execution record show under his government?

The numbers accumulated under President Masoud Pezeshkian, the elected official who governs day-to-day beneath the Supreme Leader's authority, are not ambiguous. As of July 20, 2026, the National Council of Resistance of Iran counted more than 3,920 executions carried out since Pezeshkian took office roughly two years earlier, including at least 106 women. More than 100 additional political prisoners with published names currently sit under death sentences, the majority tied to the January 2026 uprising, and human rights monitors have tracked at least 39 political executions in the weeks following the February 28 strikes that killed Khamenei — a regime absorbing a decapitation strike by accelerating its use of the gallows at home. A government that could not stop two foreign air forces from reaching its Supreme Leader has had no difficulty reaching its own citizens.

What happened when guards moved fifty women into Evin's political ward?

On July 12, 2026, prison guards transferred roughly fifty to sixty female inmates convicted of financial and non-political offenses from Qarchak Prison in Varamin into the upper floor of Ward 6 at Evin Prison in Tehran — the ward already holding female political prisoners from the January 2026 uprising, according to the Women's Committee of the NCRI and Hrana. The transfer violated Iran's own principle of separating categories of prisoners. Witnesses reported the newly arrived women and the political detainees began chanting "Death to the dictator" together; guards responded with force, dragging at least two protesting prisoners into solitary confinement. Rights monitors reported the move was the opening step in a quieter plan: exile the political women to Qarchak permanently, emptying Evin's most internationally visible ward of the prisoners the regime least wants watched.

Why do prisoners strike on Tuesdays instead of filing appeals?

On July 14, 2026, the "No to Execution Tuesdays" campaign — inmates across Iran refusing food each Tuesday to protest the state's use of capital punishment — entered its 129th consecutive week, running simultaneously across 58 prisons nationwide, according to Iran Human Rights Monitor. Two and a half years of a weekly hunger strike is not a protest that expects a legal reversal. It is a record, kept by the condemned themselves, that the killing did not stop and was not accepted quietly — a ledger running in parallel to the state's own, inside the walls the state controls.

What does a legal system built on divine authority actually protect?

Strip away the theology and the mechanism is familiar from every regime that has ever ruled by claiming a mandate no ballot could grant: when the leader's authority comes from God rather than the governed, the leader owes the governed no evidence, no appeal, no accounting. A jurist who rules as heaven's deputy cannot be voted out for the crime of being wrong, because in his own legal system he cannot, by definition, be wrong about who counts as an enemy of God. Fifty women screaming in a prison ward, a hunger strike in its third year, a twenty-four-year-old sentenced for a car ride — these are not glitches in Velayat-e Faqih. They are what the doctrine was built to produce: a state that never has to explain itself, because it has already claimed the one authority no court on earth can cross-examine.
sources: NCRI, July 20, 2026 — execution toll under President Pezeshkian
Iran Human Rights Monitor, July 14, 2026 — 'No to Execution Tuesdays,' week 129
Hrana and IranWire, July 2026 — death sentence of Arghavan Fallahi
Women's Committee of the NCRI, July 12-16, 2026 — Evin Prison women's ward transfer
Bloomberg and Washington Post, February 28, 2026 — assassination of Ali Khamenei
Irish Times, Gulf International Forum, and Stimson Center, March 2026 — succession of Mojtaba Khamenei
